title: Sarga 67
summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Then, as the night advanced, Shatrughna asked the brahmin Chyavana, the son of Bhrigu, about the extent of Lavana's strength.” and develops toward “To Indra, who was speaking thus, Mandhata replied, "O Indra, where on the surface of the earth is my rule obstructed?"”

This passage begins with “Indra informed (Shatrughna) that a demon named Lavana, son of Madhu, residing in Madhuvana, does not obey his commands, O sinless one.” and develops toward “The welfare of the worlds will be achieved by this deed done by you, and all this about the wicked Lavana has been told to you.”

This passage begins with “O best among men, the terrible and immeasurable strength of the trident, and the destruction of Mandhata which was caused by him, has also been recounted, O king.” and develops toward “O great-souled one, you will kill Lavana tomorrow morning; there is no doubt in my mind about this, and your victory will be certain, O king, when he…”

verses.sanskrit: अथ रात्र्यां प्रवृत्तायां शत्रुघ्नो भृगुनन्दनम् । पप्रच्छ च्यवनं विप्रं लवणस्य यथा बलम् ।। 7.67.1 ।।
verses.translation: Then, as the night advanced, Shatrughna asked the brahmin Chyavana, the son of Bhrigu, about the extent of Lavana's strength.

verses.sanskrit: शूलस्य च बलं ब्रह्मन्के च पूर्वं विनाशिताः । अनेन शूलमुख्येन द्वन्द्वयुद्धमुपागताः ।। 7.67.2 ।।
verses.translation: O Brahmana, Shatrughna further inquired about the strength of the trident and who had previously been destroyed by this formidable trident when they engaged in single combat.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य तद्वचनं श्रुत्वा शत्रुघ्नस्य महात्मनः । प्रत्युवाच महातेजाश्च्यवनो रघुनन्दनम् ।। 7.67.3 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard those words of the great-souled Shatrughna, the greatly effulgent Chyavana replied to the scion of Raghu.

verses.sanskrit: असङ्ख्येयानि कर्माणि यान्यस्य रघुनन्दन । इक्ष्वाकुवंशप्रभवे यद्वृत्तं तच्छृणष्व मे ।। 7.67.4 ।।
verses.translation: O scion of Raghu, listen to me about the innumerable deeds and the story of him who was born in the Ikshvaku lineage.

verses.sanskrit: अयोध्यायां पुरा राजा युवनाश्वसुतो बली । मान्धातेति स विख्यातस्त्रिषु लोकेषु वीर्यवान् ।। 7.67.5 ।।
verses.translation: Formerly in Ayodhya, there was a powerful king, the son of Yuvanashva, who was renowned as Mandhata and was valorous in the three worlds.

verses.sanskrit: स कृत्वा पृथिवीं कृत्स्नां शासने पृथिवीपतिः । सुरलोकमितो जेतुमुद्योगमकरोन्नृपः ।। 7.67.6 ।।
verses.translation: Having brought the entire earth under his rule, that king, the lord of the earth, then made an effort to conquer the world of the gods from here.

verses.sanskrit: इन्द्रस्य च भयं तीव्रं सुराणां च महात्मनाम् । मान्धातरि कृतोद्योगे देवलोकजिगीषया ।। 7.67.7 ।।
verses.translation: Intense fear arose in Indra and the great-souled gods when Mandhata made the effort with the desire to conquer the world of the gods.

verses.sanskrit: अर्धासनेन शक्रस्य राज्यार्धेन च पार्थिवः । वन्द्यमानः सुरगणैः प्रतिज्ञामध्यरोहत ।। 7.67.8 ।।
verses.translation: The king, Mandhata, who was honored with half of Indra's seat and half of his kingdom, and saluted by the hosts of gods, then undertook a challenge.

verses.sanskrit: तस्य पापमभिप्रायं विदित्वा पाकशासनः । सान्त्वपूर्वमिदं वाक्यमुवाच युवनाश्वजम् ।। 7.67.9 ।।
verses.translation: Knowing Mandhata's sinful intention, Indra, the slayer of Paka, spoke these conciliatory words to the son of Yuvanashva.

verses.sanskrit: राजा त्वं मानुषे लोके न तावत्पुरुषर्षभ । अकृत्वा पृथिवीं वश्यां देवराज्यमिहेच्छसि ।। 7.67.10 ।।
verses.translation: O best among men, you are a king in the human world, and you desire the kingdom of the gods here without first bringing the entire earth under your control.

verses.sanskrit: यदि वीर समग्रा ते मेदिनी निखिला वशे । देवराज्यं कुरुष्वेह सभृत्यबलवाहनः ।। 7.67.11 ।।
verses.translation: O hero, if your entire earth is completely under your control, then you may establish the kingdom of the gods here with your servants, army, and vehicles.

verses.sanskrit: इन्द्रमेवं ब्रुवाणं तं मान्धाता वाक्यमब्रवीत् । क्व मे शक्र प्रतिहतं शासनं पृथिवीतले ।। 7.67.12 ।।
verses.translation: To Indra, who was speaking thus, Mandhata replied, "O Indra, where on the surface of the earth is my rule obstructed?"

verses.sanskrit: तमुवाच सहस्राक्षो लवणो नाम राक्षसः । मधुपुत्रो मधुवने न ते ऽ ऽज्ञां कुरुते ऽनघ ।। 7.67.13 ।।
verses.translation: Indra informed (Shatrughna) that a demon named Lavana, son of Madhu, residing in Madhuvana, does not obey his commands, O sinless one.

verses.sanskrit: तच्छ्रुत्वा विप्रियं घोरं सहस्राक्षेण भाषितम् । व्रीडितो ऽवाङ्मुखो राजा व्याहर्तुं न शशाक ह ।। 7.67.14 ।।
verses.translation: Having heard those terrible and unpleasant words spoken by Indra, the king (Shatrughna) was ashamed and, with a downcast face, was indeed unable to speak.

verses.sanskrit: आमन्त्र्य तु सहस्राक्षं ह्रिया किञ्चिदवाङ्मुखः । पुनरेवागमच्छ्रीमानिमं लोकं नरेश्वरः ।। 7.67.15 ।।
verses.translation: And having bid farewell to Indra, the glorious lord of men (Shatrughna), still somewhat ashamed and with a downcast face, returned to this world.

verses.sanskrit: स कृत्वा हृदये ऽमर्षं सभृत्यबलवाहनः । आजगाम मधोः पुत्रं वशे कर्तुमरिन्दमः ।। 7.67.16 ।।
verses.translation: Holding indignation in his heart, the subduer of enemies (Shatrughna) came with his servants, army, and vehicles to bring Madhu's son (Lavana) under his control.

verses.sanskrit: स काङ्क्षमाणो लवणं युद्धाय पुरुषर्षभः । दूतं सम्प्रेषयामास सकाशं लवणस्य हि ।। 7.67.17 ।।
verses.translation: Desiring battle with Lavana, the best among men (Shatrughna) indeed sent a messenger to Lavana's presence.

verses.sanskrit: स गत्वा विप्रियाण्याह बहूनि मधुनः सुतम् । वदन्तमेवं तं दूतं भक्षयामास राक्षसः ।। 7.67.18 ।।
verses.translation: Having gone and spoken many unpleasant things to Madhu's son, the demon Lavaṇa devoured that messenger who was speaking thus.

verses.sanskrit: चिरायमाणे दूते तु राजा क्रोधसमन्वितः । अर्दयामास तद्रक्षः शरवृष्ट्या समन्ततः ।। 7.67.19 ।।
verses.translation: When the messenger delayed, the king, filled with anger, attacked that demon Lavaṇa from all sides with a shower of arrows.

verses.sanskrit: ततः प्रहस्य तद्रक्षः शूलं जग्राह पाणिना । वधाय सानुबन्धस्य मुमोचायुधमुत्तमम् ।। 7.67.20 ।।
verses.translation: Then, that demon Lavaṇa laughed loudly, took the excellent trident in his hand, and released that supreme weapon to kill the king along with his retinue.

verses.sanskrit: तच्छूलं दीप्यमानं तु सभृत्यबलवाहनम् । भस्मीकृत्वा नृपं भूयो लवणस्यागमत्करम् ।। 7.67.21 ।।
verses.translation: That brightly shining trident, having reduced the king along with his servants, army, and vehicles to ashes, then returned to Lavaṇa's hand.

verses.sanskrit: एवं स राजा सुमहान्हतः सबलवाहनः । शूलस्य तु बलं सौम्य अप्रमेयमनुत्तमम् ।। 7.67.22 ।।
verses.translation: Thus, that very great king was killed along with his army and vehicles; indeed, O gentle one, the power of that trident is immeasurable and unsurpassed.

verses.sanskrit: श्वः प्रभाते तु लवणं वधिष्यसि न संशयः । अगृहीतायुधं क्षिप्रं ध्रुवो हि विजयस्तव ।। 7.67.23 ।।
verses.translation: Tomorrow morning, you will certainly kill Lavana, and your victory is assured, especially when he has not taken up his weapon.

verses.sanskrit: लोकानां स्वस्ति चैव स्यात्कृते कर्मणि च त्वया । एतत्ते सर्वमाख्यातं लवणस्य दुरात्मनः ।। 7.67.24 ।।
verses.translation: The welfare of the worlds will be achieved by this deed done by you, and all this about the wicked Lavana has been told to you.

verses.sanskrit: शूलस्य च बलं घोरमप्रमेयं नरर्षभ । विनाशश्चैव मान्धातुर्यत्तेनाभूच्च पार्थिव ।। 7.67.25 ।।
verses.translation: O best among men, the terrible and immeasurable strength of the trident, and the destruction of Mandhata which was caused by him, has also been recounted, O king.

verses.sanskrit: त्वं श्वः प्रभाते लवणं महात्मन्वधिष्यसे नात्र तु संशयो मे । शूलं विना निर्गतमामिषार्थे ध्रुवो जयस्ते भविता नरेन्द्र ।। 7.67.26 ।।
verses.translation: O great-souled one, you will kill Lavana tomorrow morning; there is no doubt in my mind about this, and your victory will be certain, O king, when he has gone out without his trident for the sake of food. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे सप्तषष्टितमः सर्गः ।। 67 ।। Thus ends the sixty-seventh sarga of Uttara Kanda of the holy Ramayana the first epic composed by sage Valmiki.
